In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

/opt/anaconda3/lib/python3.8/site-packages/pandas/core/computation/expressions.py:20: UserWarning: Pandas requires version '2.7.3' or newer of 'numexpr' (version '2.7.1' currently installed).
  from pandas.core.computation.check import NUMEXPR_INSTALLED

Read in the data using pandas to explore the dataset before producing a new csv that will be used for the regression.

In [2]:
train = pd.read_csv("train.csv")
train_nums = train.select_dtypes(include=['int64','float64'])
train_nums = train_nums.drop(columns=["Id"])

test = pd.read_csv("test.csv")
test_nums = test.select_dtypes(include=['int64','float64'])
test_nums = test_nums.drop(columns=["Id"])

In [5]:
# DONT NEED TO RUN FOR REGRESSION JUST COOL PLOT
import seaborn as sns
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
# Show the correlation plot between all the features
fig,ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 10))
sns.heatmap(train_nums.corr(), ax=ax, linewidths=0.05,cmap="magma")
plt.show()
In [6]:
# DATA PLOTS NOT NEEDED FOR REGRESSION
fig, axs = plt.subplots(36)
cnt = 0

for col in train_nums.columns:
    if col not in ["SalePrice"]:
        axs[cnt].plot(train_nums[col], train_nums.SalePrice,'o',color='Red')
        axs[cnt].set_xlabel(f"{col}", fontsize=20)
        axs[cnt].set_ylabel("Sale Price", fontsize=20)
        axs[cnt].set_title(f"{col} vs Sale Price", fontsize=20)
        cnt+=1
fig.set_size_inches(30, 87)
plt.show()

From the dataset many of the numeric columns are still categorical data which will not be very useful to the elastic net model. These columns should not be used in the regression.

In [7]:
# Categorical Data that will not be used
catcols = ["MSSubClass", "OverallQual", "OverallCond", "YearBuilt", "YearRemodAdd", 
           "BsmtFullBath", "BsmtHalfBath", "FullBath", "HalfBath", "BedroomAbvGr", "KitchenAbvGr",
          "TotRmsAbvGrd", "Fireplaces", "GarageYrBlt", "GarageCars", "WoodDeckSF", "MoSold", "YrSold"]
train_data = train_nums.drop(catcols, axis='columns')
train_data

Gradient Descent Implementation of Elastic Net¶

In [14]:
def cost(X, y, weights, alpha, ratio):
    # Sourced from Scikit for accurate comparison
    n = X.shape[0]
    
    y_pred = np.dot(X, weights)
    mse = (1/(2*n)) * np.sum((y_pred-y)**2)
    
    l1 = alpha * ratio * np.sum(np.abs(weights))
    l2 = (1-alpha) * ratio * np.sum(weights**2)
    
    return mse + l1 + l2
In [15]:
def gradients(X, y, weights, alpha, ratio, residue):
    # Calculates current gradient of the cost function
    n = X.shape[0]
    
    mse = (1/n) * np.dot(X.T, residue)
    l1 = alpha * ratio * np.sign(weights)
    l2 = alpha * (1-ratio) * 2 * weights
    
    return mse + l1 + l2
In [16]:
def fit_grad_descent(X, y, alpha, l_ratio, lr, tol):
    num_rows, num_features = X.shape
    
    coefs = np.ones(num_features).T
    intercept = 0
    
    for i in range(1000):
        y_pred = intercept + np.dot(X, coefs)
        residue = (y_pred-y)
        grad = gradients(X, y, coefs, alpha, l_ratio, residue)
        
        prev_coefs = coefs.copy()
        
        coefs -= lr*grad
        intercept -= lr*(1/num_rows)*np.sum(residue)
        
        if np.linalg.norm(coefs - prev_coefs, ord=2) < tol:
            print(f'Converged in {i+1} iterations')
            return intercept, coefs
    
    return intercept, coefs
In [17]:
# Normalize all features to mean 0 so all features have same scale
train_mean = np.mean(X, axis=0)
train_std = np.std(X, axis=0)

X_normed = (X - train_mean)/ train_std

# X_normed = np.c_[np.ones(X.shape[0]), X_normed]
In [18]:
# Calculate to coefficents for gradient descent method first column
grad_intercept, grad_coefs = fit_grad_descent(X_normed, Y, 1, .5, .15, 1e-5)
grad_coefs

Converged in 80 iterations

y_pred = grad_intercept +  np.dot(X_normed, grad_coefs)

grad_error
Out[20]:
31084.48502956484

Coordinate Descent implentation of Elastic Net¶

In [21]:
def soft_eqn(rho, lam):
    if rho < -lam:
        return rho + lam
    elif rho > lam:
        return rho - lam
    else:
        return 0
In [22]:
def fit_coord_descent(X, y, alpha, l_ratio, tol):
    num_rows, num_features = X.shape
    
    coefs = np.zeros(num_features)
    
    lambda_1 =  l_ratio * alpha
    lambda_2 = (1-l_ratio) * alpha
    
    intercept = 0
    
    for i in range(40000):
        prev_coefs = coefs.copy()
        
        for j in range(num_features):
            residue = y - (np.dot(X, coefs) - coefs[j] * X[:, j])
            
            rhotemp = np.dot(X[:, j], residue)
            coefs[j] = soft_eqn(rhotemp, lambda_1) / (np.dot(X[:, j].T, X[:,j]) + lambda_2)
            
        if np.sum(np.abs(coefs - prev_coefs)) < tol:
            print(f'Converged in {i+1} iterations')
            return coefs
        
    print(f'Did not Converge')
    return coefs
In [23]:
# Calculate to coefficents for coordinate descent method first column is bias term generated from 1s column
# Need lot more iterations than gradient due to 19 features and nature of coordinate descent
X_normed = np.c_[np.ones(X.shape[0]), X_normed]
final_coefs = fit_coord_descent(X_normed, Y, 1, .5, .002)
final_coefs

Converged in 12235 iterations
Out[23]:
array([180368.54579674,  -3045.90531431,   3866.91983298,   9377.26634339,
        10251.84022989,    425.853913  ,   3219.04699252,  13980.9704515 ,
        10911.64427989,  13905.32132693,  -1923.92733845,  19157.60390039,
        20401.22344726,   2544.5068514 ,  -4555.00259117,   1188.62207084,
         2288.49469262,  -4244.10150932,  -1089.62302041])
In [24]:
y_pred = np.dot(X_normed, final_coefs)
coord_error = (1/Y.shape[0])*np.sum(np.abs(y_pred - Y))
coord_error
Out[24]:
28347.636176850254

Both methods converge to ~same weights/coefficents showing consistency irrespective of method. Do note during testing gradient descent did peform better than coordinate descent¶

Sklearn Implementation¶

In [25]:
from sklearn.linear_model import ElasticNet
In [26]:
skmodel = ElasticNet(alpha=1, l1_ratio=.5, random_state=42)
X_normed = (X - train_mean)/ train_std
skmodel.fit(X_normed, Y)
Out[26]:
ElasticNet(alpha=1, random_state=42)

Sklearn Intercept: 180444.0142259414, Coordinate Descent: 180368.5457967378, and Grad Descent Intercept 180443.60698021352

Testing hyperparameter tuning to find optimal alpha and mixing parameter¶

In [31]:
alphas = np.linspace(0, 1.0, num=40)
ratios = np.linspace(0, .9, num=40)

curmin = 100000
bestalpha = 0
bestratio = 0

# Rudimentry implementation of gridsearch for hyperparameters
for a in alphas:
    cnt=0
    for r in ratios:
        grad_intercept, grad_coefs = fit_grad_descent(X_normed, Y, a, r, .15, 1e-5)
        y_pred = grad_intercept +  np.dot(X_normed, grad_coefs)
        cur_error = (1/Y.shape[0])*np.sum(np.abs(y_pred - Y))
        if cur_error < curmin:
            bestalpha = a
            bestratio = r
            curmin = cur_error

In [32]:
print(f'Best alpha: {bestalpha}, Best Mixing Parameter: {bestratio}')

Best alpha: 0.23076923076923075, Best Mixing Parameter: 0.8769230769230769
In [33]:
alphas = np.linspace(0, 5, num=40)

coefs_list = []
error_list = []

for a in alphas:
    grad_intercept, grad_coefs = fit_grad_descent(X_normed, Y, a, .5, .15, 1e-5)
    coefs_list.append(grad_coefs)
    y_pred = grad_intercept +  np.dot(X_normed, grad_coefs)
    cur_error = (1/Y.shape[0])*np.sum(np.abs(y_pred - Y))
    error_list.append(cur_error)

Converged in 476 iterations

In [34]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14,5))

ax.plot(alphas, coefs_list, linewidth =2.0);
ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_xlabel('alpha (log-scale)', fontsize=14)
ax.set_ylabel('Coeffecients', fontsize=14)
Out[34]:
Text(0, 0.5, 'Coeffecients')
In [36]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14,5))

ax.plot(alphas, error_list, linewidth =2.0);
ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_xlabel('alpha (log-scale)', fontsize=14)
ax.set_ylabel('error', fontsize=14)
Out[36]:
Text(0, 0.5, 'error')
